Looking at the V4 hints, `EmployeeDbAccess` appears in the code examples but isn't mentioned in the hints—though that's a test-specific class rather than a framework class, so it's probably fine. `SqlResultSet` is properly documented in s3 and s5.

Looking at the actual example more carefully, I see the type comment row `// CHAR(5)  VARCHAR(64)   BOOLEAN` is positioned as its own line between the column names and the data rows, not integrated into the column name row as the documentation implies. findings.description: The description '2行目: カラム名（`// <型>` でデータ型コメントを付与可能）' has two problems. First, it misplaces the type comment as part of the column-name row (row 2), but in the source example the `// CHAR(5)  VARCHAR(64)   BOOLEAN` row appears as a separate data row after the column names — not combined with them. Second, the source never explicitly documents this `//` type-comment syntax; it only appears silently in the example table without any accompanying explanation, so presenting it as an explicitly described optional feature ('付与可能') is not grounded in source text.
findings.source_evidence: Source EXPECTED_TABLE format description lists only '1行目: EXPECTED_TABLE=<テーブル名>', '2行目: 確認対象テーブルのカラム名', '3行目〜: 期待する値'. The example shows `// CHAR(5)  VARCHAR(64)   BOOLEAN` as a row inside the table body (after the RST `===` separator), not merged into the column-name row and not explained anywhere in the surrounding text.

content.text:
**Summary**

One minor finding:

- **s9 — fabrication (minor)**: The phrase `2行目: カラム名（// <型> でデータ型コメントを付与可能）` misrepresents the type-comment row in two ways:
  1. It places the `//` type info within the column-name row (row 2), but the source example shows `// CHAR(5)  VARCHAR(64)   BOOLEAN` as its own separate row in the data body, after the column header.
  2. The source never explains this syntax at all — it only appears in the example table without any textual description. Presenting it as a documented optional feature ("付与可能") fabricates an explicit rule that isn't in the source.

Everything else checks out: all API methods, warnings, code examples, SETUP_TABLE/LIST_MAP/EXPECTED_TABLE/EXPECTED_COMPLETE_TABLE distinctions, the commit-required rule for update tests, the primary-key-cannot-be-omitted constraint, and the image asset references are all faithfully represented.

result:
**Summary**

One minor finding:

- **s9 — fabrication (minor)**: The phrase `2行目: カラム名（// <型> でデータ型コメントを付与可能）` misrepresents the type-comment row in two ways:
  1. It places the `//` type info within the column-name row (row 2), but the source example shows `// CHAR(5)  VARCHAR(64)   BOOLEAN` as its own separate row in the data body, after the column header.
  2. The source never explains this syntax at all — it only appears in the example table without any textual description. Presenting it as a documented optional feature ("付与可能") fabricates an explicit rule that isn't in the source.

Everything else checks out: all API methods, warnings, code examples, SETUP_TABLE/LIST_MAP/EXPECTED_TABLE/EXPECTED_COMPLETE_TABLE distinctions, the commit-required rule for update tests, the primary-key-cannot-be-omitted constraint, and the image asset references are all faithfully represented.

findings.description: The description '2行目: カラム名（`// <型>` でデータ型コメントを付与可能）' has two problems. First, it misplaces the type comment as part of the column-name row (row 2), but in the source example the `// CHAR(5)  VARCHAR(64)   BOOLEAN` row appears as a separate data row after the column names — not combined with them. Second, the source never explicitly documents this `//` type-comment syntax; it only appears silently in the example table without any accompanying explanation, so presenting it as an explicitly described optional feature ('付与可能') is not grounded in source text.
findings.source_evidence: Source EXPECTED_TABLE format description lists only '1行目: EXPECTED_TABLE=<テーブル名>', '2行目: 確認対象テーブルのカラム名', '3行目〜: 期待する値'. The example shows `// CHAR(5)  VARCHAR(64)   BOOLEAN` as a row inside the table body (after the RST `===` separator), not merged into the column-name row and not explained anywhere in the surrounding text.
